metadata.title: Social Services in the Cathedral Homes Area
metadata.author: Gabriel Pavia Nolla
metadata.subtitle: How Public Service Organizations Helped Shape and Provide for the Community

caption.text: Before the creation of Cathedral Homes, one of the first public housing developments in Boston, there existed a lower income community that characterized the [South End]|( https://sah-archipedia.org/essays/MA-01-0001-0009) during the first half of the 20th century. This [community]( https://socialwelfare.library.vcu.edu/settlement-houses/south-end-house-boston-ma/) were largely made up of immigrants or the children of immigrants The buildings that occupied this area largely consisted of brick houses that were used as either boarding, tenement, or settlement housing.
caption.title: The Cathedral Homes Area in 1917 Atlas

caption.text: One set of buildings that encapsulate the type of housing that existed at Cathedral Homes are the row brick buildings on 13-37 East Canton street. These are excellent examples of the type of crowded and unkept living conditions that were found at the heart of the Cathedral Homes area.
caption.title: 13-37 East Canton Street in 1917 Atlas

caption.text: From the front these buildings give a cleaner appearance, but once the context that these homes were tenement housing is revealed, the space seems far more cramped considering the amount of people that lived in each of these buildings.
caption.title: Tenement houses, 35-37 East Canton Street in 1922

caption.text: The back of these buildings reveals a clearer picture of the poor upkeep and crowded nature of these tenement houses.
caption.title: Tenement houses, Rear Exterior, 35-37 East Canton Street

caption.text: While the appearances of the tenement houses in the heart of what is now Cathedral Homes presents a rougher and less appealing urban environment, residences that stood on the periphery of the residential area along East Brookline Street and in front of Franklin Park retained more architectural features like the entrance stoops and iron hand rails. These buildings recall back to the time before many of the wealthier Boston residents [left the South End]( https://sah-archipedia.org/essays/MA-01-0001-0009) to move to the newly created Back Bay neighborhood in the 1870s.
caption.title: Houses on the Periphery of the Community as seen in 1917 Atlas

caption.text: This picture was shared on the Vintage Boston Facebook Group in which a member is sharing a picture of his grandfather and great-uncle in 1922 standing in front of a house in the South End. The contributor mentions that his grandfather lived at 22 East Brookline Street in 1922, the contributor does mistakenly refer to this part of Boston as Roxbury rather than the South End. The characteristic architecture of the building in the back does have the characteristic features of a South End House. Nevertheless, there is little primary source material that could be found which corroborates that this was indeed the address the contributor’s residences lived in, with his last name having no record in the Boston directories of 1922.
caption.title: Typical South End Brick Residence Home that is Presumably on East Brookline Street

caption.text: Additionally, the houses on Atlascope on 22 East Brookline Street do appear to have that entrance stoop as seen in the picture. Therefore, there is a good chance that the building in the back is a now demolished residential home that stood in Cathedral Homes that could even be the 22 East Brookline residence.
caption.title: Evidence of the Stoop Seen on a 1922 Atlas

caption.text: To further understand the community that existed before Cathedral Homes, one must look at the importance of religion that played in the lives of the residents. In fact, Cathedral Homes gets its name from the neighboring [Cathedral of Holy Cross]( https://sah-archipedia.org/buildings/MA-01-SE12) which provided an anchor to the predominantly catholic community in the area. Many of the residents in the community also tended to be immigrants or the descendants of immigrants from predominantly Catholic countries like Ireland and Italy. Therefore, the Catholic church ended provided a center of worship, community, and education through the catholic school it hosted next to the church.
caption.title: Religion in the Community

caption.text: The Cathedral of Holy Cross is the largest Catholic church in New England. It is of the early English Gothic style, and it reflects greatly on the large catholic population in the South End.
caption.title: View of Cathedral of Holy Cross from Washington Street in the 1920

caption.text: The Cathedral of the Holy Cross continues to exist largely unchanged. The cathedral continues to serve those living in the Cathedral Homes community who are part of the Catholic faith.
caption.title: Cathedral of Holy Cross in 2026

caption.text: To recap, the community at Cathedral Homes before redevelopment was predominantly catholic and lower income. Many of the residents who lived here had to find ways to balance long working hours, taking care of their families, and at times needed access to other forms of social services.
caption.title: Where to Work and How to Balance it

caption.text: While living conditions were poor in many of the boarding and tenement houses that existed, many of the residents took advantage of the neighboring factories, businesses, and mills for employment opportunities
caption.title: Neighboring Employment Opportunities

caption.text: Lumber Yards and other woodworking industries populated in the areas near Cathedral Homes. This form of industry started thrived in the South End, especially near the waterfront via the [Fort Point Channel, South Bay, and Roxbury Canal]( https://sah-archipedia.org/essays/MA-01-0001-0009-0001).
caption.title: A Popular Industry: Lumber Yards

caption.text: While not exactly a lumber yard, Perkins Wood Working Co, which was located at 38 Wareham Street, is an example of the many wood-working companies that would have employed nearby residents and worked with the surrounding lumber yards.
caption.title: Perkins Wood Working Co Advertisement in 1916 Boston Directory

caption.text: Most industry in this area started to dissipate once more, the waterfront was filled in midway through the 20th century. However, by 1938 there was evidence that the lumber industry had started to shrink. Two of the lumber yards that were next to St. Helena’s had been bought and incorporated. Lumber yards and woodworking shops like Perkins between Malden and Wareham Street were now gone.
caption.title: Lumber Yards Disappearing in the 1938 Atlas

caption.text: That is not to say that all lumber mills and woodworking shops in the region had entirely disappeared by 1938.
caption.title: Surviving Lumber Industry in 1938 Atlas

caption.text: While the industries were close to the Cathedral Homes area community, long working hours and low pay made it hard for many in the community to make ends meet. At the time there was a lack of social safety nets offered by the government, and as a result many in the community had to turn to private organizations for assistance.
caption.title: Social Services

caption.text: For low-income immigrant communities in the South End, assimilation and balancing home responsibilities was a challenge. The [South End House Association](https://socialwelfare.library.vcu.edu/settlement-houses/south-end-house-boston-ma/) attempted to provide immigrants and low-income households services such as helping preserve family life, promote cultural and intellectual development, provide employment counseling, daycare, and tutoring in the English language. The Association has some religious roots, but ultimately as founder [William J. Tucker]( https://books.google.com/books?id=UNUJAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA125#v=onepage&q&f=false)  descripted the association’s aims, “The whole aim and mtive is religious, but the method is educational rather than evangelistic.” This building, while not situated in the Cathedral Homes area, primarily served its residents. Additionally, the [South Bay Union](https://archivesspace.library.northeastern.edu/repositories/2/resources/910) building that is attached to the Association’s was used as a neighborhood center and townhall which also provided a Well Baby Clinic.
caption.title: The South End House Association and South Bay Union

caption.text: Within the Cathedral Homes area, South End House Association established two single and working women residences that offered cheap housing opportunities. The women residences helped balance responsibilities thanks to the services offered.
caption.title: 43-45 East Canton Women’s Residences

caption.text: Unfortunately, by 1922 the women's South End settlement houses ceased to exist in the community.
caption.title: South End House Association’s Women's Settlement Houses closes in the Cathedral Homes area.

caption.text: Although the women’s settlement houses may have left, the South End House Association and the South Bay Union Continued to operate well into the last atlas before the removal of the community in the Cathedral Homes area.
caption.title: South End House Association in 1938 Atlas

caption.text: In the present, all building were demolished and now there is a basketball court where residents of Cathedral Homes can now enjoy.
caption.title: Basketball Court Located Where the South End House Association and South Bay Union had Existed (2025)

caption.text: In addition to the Settlement Houses in the area, religious institutions like the Salvation Army provided social services in the area.
caption.title: 1917 Atlas View of the Cathedral Homes Area

caption.text: Although not reflective of the Catholic community that lived in the area, the [Salvation Army]( https://easternusa.salvationarmy.org/massachusetts/boston-south-end/who-we-are) considers itself a church that preaches the gospel and meet the needs in the name of God without discrimination. In this community, the Salvation army operated the People’s Palace Hotel.
caption.title: The Salvation Army’s People’s Palace Hotel

caption.text: Within this article, it details the early plans that the Salvation Army had for the construction of the hotel at Washington and Brookline Street. The building did not function just as a normal hotel, rather its purpose is both to serve as cheap hotel for workingmen, and as one of the Salvation Army’s headquarters in Boston. Additionally, the hotel wants to offer its occupants plentiful meeting space and access to cheap resources that would help them avoid living on the streets.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1905 Article on the Salvation Army’s Site Purchase for the People’s Hotel

caption.text: People’s Palace Hotel in 1909: Here is a picture of the People’s Palace Hotel when it was first built. At the time the architectural designed was described as being [“modern in every particular, and as nearly fireproof as possible.”]( https://www.proquest.com/docview/500397722/D9E062822D8847C6PQ/1?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) The design also took inspiration from a similar Salvation Army hotel that was built in Cleveland.
caption.title: People’s Palace Hotel in 1909

caption.text: The Salvation Army continues to house its headquarters right next to where the original structure stood, today. The original building was demolished along with the rest of the buildings to make way for the construction of Cathedral Homes Public Housing Project.
caption.title: The Salvation Army Headquarters in Cathedral Homes in 2026

caption.text: Having moved here at some point between 1917 and 1922, the Ladies' Catholic Club represents a Catholic religious organization that operated Settlement Houses within the Cathedral Homes area. The club was located at 18 East Brookline Street, just one house apart from the suspected home of the Vintage Boston Facebook Contributor's 1922 home.
caption.title: Ladies' Catholic Club East Brookline Street Settlement House in 1922 Atlas

caption.text: Although this was before the club moved into the Cathedral Homes area, the article covers what type of services the club offered in the South End. This includes teaching young girls various important trades that could be used in the workforce.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1916 Article on the Ladies' Catholic Club

caption.text: In this article, the Ladies' Catholic Club is noted for hosting a "Irish Night" at the East Brookline Settlement House. Here, the club provided an illustrated lecture on Ireland. Thus, showing the club is providing one of the many social services that settlement houses tended to provide in the South End. In this case they are both educating and promoting cultural development to its beneficiaries.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1927 Article on Ladies' Catholic Club "Irish Night."

caption.text: Similar to the South End House Association, the Ladies' Catholic Club continued to operate in the area until the demolition and construction of Cathedral Homes in the 1940s. The Club’s existence in area demonstrates that its services that were directed at women actually replaced the void left by the disappearance of the South End House Association’s Women Residences. It also shows the continued importance of religious social services to provide recourses for lower income communities in the South End.
caption.title: Ladies' Catholic Club Continues in 1938 Atlas

caption.text: As mentioned previously, the creation of Cathedral Homes completely changed the landscape of the area. In this Atlas, the large, overcrowded nature of this urban environment is evident with the large amount of row brick houses that existed in such a small area.
caption.title: Final Atlas (1938) before the Cathedral Homes Project

caption.text: Today, this looks completely different, with the row brick houses being replaced by tall apartment buildings that provide open spaces and greenery to the residents of this community. Despite the increased gentrification that has occurred in the South End, the Cathedral Homes community continues to house some of Boston’s most economically vulnerable population. The creation of the [Cathedral Homes Public Housing Project](https://sah-archipedia.org/buildings/MA-01-SE13) is a remnant of the city of Boston’s racialized segregation through the creation of public housing for African Americans.
caption.title: Cathedral Homes in 2025 Aerial Imagery

caption.text: Where the Cathedral School once stood, a different type of educational service is now offered.
caption.title: Cathedral School in 1938 Atlas

caption.text: Head Start and Children's Services is part of two programs which offer day care and educational services to children between the ages of 3 to 5 lower income families who qualify. The organization that runs these programs [ABCD](https://bostonabcd.org/about-2/) is a nonprofit human services organization that seeks to help residents progress from poverty to stability to success.
caption.title: ABCD: Head Start and Children Service's at 597 Harrison Ave. in 2026

caption.text: Overall, the sharp contrast between what Cathedral Homes looks like today and what it once was can be jarring judging simply by architectural design. Nevertheless, the story of this place before the development of the public housing project is not one that is all to different from the present. While now there are more government assistance programs that were offered to low income communities than there were in the past, the needs for community and social services remain the same. Religious institutions and charitable non-profit organizations have always characterized this place for the work they have done in protecting and serving those in need. Private or public there will always be those who are willing to help others be it someone who resides in the community or someone who lives elsewhere. Some continue to exist in the community, the Salvation Army, others are easy to be forgotten because their impact appears to be gone. However, understanding the origins of place can help us better situate the present and in what ways they correlate.
caption.title: Picture of Cathedral Homes 2026
